context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help figuring out this reflection prompt on corruption and development tbh. I’m kinda stuck on how to connect the “grease the wheel” idea with state legitimacy and the Hong Kong example.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you've already identified the key tension in the prompt. Before we dig into the connection, tell me: what do you understand by the 'grease the wheel' thesis? What is it actually claiming about corruption?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core tension in the prompt well. Rather than scaffolding too heavily at the start, I should ask them what they already understand about the 'grease the wheel' thesis to assess their baseline and let them build from there using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I think it means corruption can sometimes make a slow or rigid system work faster, like bribery helps ppl get around bad bureaucracy? But idk if the claim is that corruption is actually good, or just that it shows the formal system is failing.
exchanges.tutor: That second interpretation you raised is really interesting — that corruption signals a failing formal system. Can you push that idea further? What does it suggest about the *state itself* if citizens routinely have to work around its formal institutions to get things done?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuitive grasp of the thesis. They've also spontaneously raised a more nuanced interpretation — that corruption signals a failing formal system — which is exactly the kind of insight that connects to state legitimacy. I should push them to develop this second idea further, as it's the more analytically rich thread and directly connects to the prompt's question about what corruption 'signifies' about the state.

exchanges.student: I guess it means the state looks weak or maybe not fully legit, bc its official rules aren’t really what ppl rely on in practice. Like the real system becomes informal networks instead of public institutions, which feels bad for development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight. Now, Hong Kong is held up as a case where corruption was actually *rooted out* successfully. Given what you just said about informal networks replacing weak institutions — what does Hong Kong's success suggest had to change for that to happen?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a strong connection between corruption and state legitimacy. They've landed on a key insight about the gap between formal and informal systems. Now I should push them to connect this to the Hong Kong case, which is the counter-example in the prompt, to create productive tension in their thinking.

exchanges.student: Maybe the state had to become more credible and actually enforce rules consistently, not just punish a few ppl. Ngl I’m guessing, but I think stronger institutions + public trust had to replace the informal workaround system.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. So now put these two ideas in tension: the 'grease the wheel' thesis suggests corruption can be functional, but Hong Kong shows it can be eliminated. Does Hong Kong's success *disprove* the grease the wheel thesis, or does it say something more nuanced about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and building toward a coherent argument about institutions and legitimacy. They've connected the dots between enforcement, credibility, and public trust. Now I should push them to bring this back into tension with the 'grease the wheel' thesis — specifically, does Hong Kong's success undermine or complicate that thesis? This will help them develop the analytical core of their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: I think it’s more nuanced tbh. Maybe corruption can seem “functional” in a badly designed system, but Hong Kong suggests that’s not a real long-term solution since better institutions can do the same job without corruption.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a genuinely nuanced position. Now take it one step further: given everything you've said, what's your answer to the opening question of the prompt — is corruption-free development *ever* possible?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong analytical point — that corruption is a symptom of institutional failure, not a genuine functional alternative. This is a nuanced position that directly addresses the prompt. Now I should push them toward the first part of the prompt — whether corruption-free development is ever possible — to help them build a complete argument for their reflection.

exchanges.student: I’d say maybe not perfectly corruption-free, but low-corruption development def seems possible. Like the real point is whether the state can build legit institutions so development doesn’t depend on corruption to function.
exchanges.tutor: You've now got a clear position on all three parts of the prompt. I'd encourage you to stop here and try drafting a paragraph or two integrating these ideas. What's your central argument going to be, and how will you open the piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed substantive positions on all three parts of the prompt. They have enough material to start writing. Given we're about 6 exchanges in and they have coherent ideas on all three questions, I should push them to start integrating their thinking into actual written content rather than continuing to develop ideas verbally.

exchanges.student: I think my main argument is that corruption isn’t some unavoidable feature of “Asian” development, but more a sign of weak or mistrusted institutions. I’m not sure how to open it tho — maybe with the idea that calling corruption “functional” confuses short-term coping with legit state capacity?
exchanges.tutor: That sounds like a promising direction. There's only one way to find out if it works — write it. Try drafting your opening, then share it and we can discuss what's working and what needs development.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a strong central argument and is already thinking about their opening move. The opening idea they've proposed is analytically sharp. Rather than validating it too enthusiastically or helping them write it, I should ask them to actually write it — they have enough to start drafting, and my role is not to pre-approve their language before they write.

exchanges.student: Ok, I’ll try: “Corruption is often treated as an unavoidable or even useful part of development, especially through claims that in parts of Asia it can ‘grease the wheels’ of growth. However, this view mistakes a short-term adaptation to weak institutions for a sustainable model of development.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a good start — your central argument is clear. Now, how are you planning to bring in the Hong Kong case and the question of state legitimacy in the body of your piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid opening that captures their central argument well. It's clear and analytically grounded. Rather than praising too much or suggesting language changes, I should push them forward — specifically toward how they'll develop the argument about state legitimacy and the Hong Kong case in the body of the piece. I should not suggest any language or edits.

exchanges.student: I think I’d use Hong Kong to show corruption isn’t culturally fixed, and then connect legitimacy by saying when ppl trust formal institutions, they don’t need informal payments as much. Does that sound too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor: It's not too simplistic — but ask yourself: have you fully addressed the 'Asian distinctiveness' claim? The prompt is specifically asking you to engage with that framing. What is that claim actually asserting, and what does your argument say about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some self-doubt but their instinct is actually analytically sound. I should push back on the self-doubt gently and challenge them to think about whether there's anything missing from their argument — specifically the 'Asian distinctiveness' claim which they haven't fully interrogated yet. This will help them produce a more complete reflection.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I prob need to say that “Asian distinctiveness” claims corruption fits local norms or helped rapid growth, but I don’t really buy that as an essential cultural trait. I think my argument is more that political/institutional conditions matter way more than some vague regional culture.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong analytical stance that will hold up well in your piece. You've got clear positions on all three parts of the prompt now. Go write the full draft — keep the 700-800 word limit in mind and make sure all three threads (the grease the wheel thesis, Hong Kong, and state legitimacy) are woven together. Share it when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed strong positions on all three parts of the prompt and has enough material for a complete reflection. We're at exchange 9. I should affirm their analytical direction without giving them language, and push them to complete their draft, reminding them of the word count and the need to integrate all these threads.
